nativeRange.habitat: The Red Sea Moray inhabits shallow coastal coral and rocky reefs from around 1 to 40 m depth, sheltering in caves and crevices during the day.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small fishes, shrimps, squid, and other small marine animals; in captivity generally accepts meaty frozen foods such as silversides, krill, and shrimp offered on a feeding stick. Typically fed two to three substantial meals per week rather than daily.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 2450x1633. Whole coiled eel on sand in Ras Muhammad, Egypt; sharp, well-lit, shows the pale lilac-grey body and peppered dark spots on the head. Best full-animal representative.
Diego Delso, 8688x5792. Vivid lilac-purple head emerging beside soft coral, Oman; tack sharp with the diagnostic geometric dark-dash markings clearly shown. Head/neck crop.

Diego Delso, 3659x3659. Sharp head portrait on sand, Zanzibar; lilac-to-yellow coloration with the geometric dashed pattern that gives the Geometric Moray its name. Head crop, different angle for variety.

summary: The Red Sea Moray is a large moray eel from the Indian Ocean, reaching around 65 cm, with a lilac to grey body patterned in brown and rows of dark spots on the head. It is generally considered suitable for advanced aquarists only, requiring a very large, escape-proof system.
careNotes:
The Red Sea Moray is more widely known in scientific literature as the Geometric Moray, a name that reflects the pattern of dark geometric markings along its body. Hobbyists occasionally encounter trade listings for a 'Red Sea Moray' that actually refer to Gymnothorax javanicus — a completely different species capable of reaching around 3 metres. That species, the Giant Moray, is not closely associated with the Red Sea, so if a listing combines the 'Red Sea Moray' name with a juvenile of extreme size, it is worth confirming the scientific name before purchasing.

In the aquarium, the Red Sea Moray tends to be primarily nocturnal and reclusive, spending the day wedged into caves and crevices and emerging at dusk to hunt. A 2,000-litre tank with a minimum length of 200 cm is generally advised for an adult specimen, reflecting the eel's adult size of up to 65 cm and its need for multiple secure hiding places. Lifespan in the wild has been reported at up to around 30 years; captive animals typically live shorter lives.

Like all large morays, the Red Sea Moray is widely considered an escape specialist, and a tightly-fitting, gap-free aquarium cover is generally regarded as essential. Feeding is best done with a long feeding stick rather than by hand, given its powerful jaws and the risk of infection from a bite. It is generally not considered reef-safe and will readily consume small fish, shrimp, and small crustaceans. Strong biological filtration and an oversized protein skimmer are generally advised to handle the heavy waste load produced by a large carnivore.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the originally-assigned LiveAquaria 'Red Sea Moray Eel - Juvenile' page identifies the fish as Gymnothorax javanicus (Giant Moray), which conflicts with the target scientific name Gymnothorax griseus; FishBase identification of the 'Red Sea Moray / Geometric Moray' as G. griseus is the canonical record used here. Source of taxonomy, native range, max size, temperature envelope, and diet/trophic level.

additional.name: AquaInfo (aquainfo.nl)
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (65 cm), minimum tank size (≥2000 L), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), family Muraenidae, native range (Western Indian Ocean incl. Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf), nocturnal/reclusive temperament, and the not-reef-safe assessment. Also cited 'up to 30 years' wild lifespan.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Independent confirmation of 2000 L minimum tank, 65 cm max length, 24.6–29.3°C temperature envelope, Muraenidae/Anguilliformes taxonomy, Indo-Pacific (Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Indian Ocean, Madagascar, Mauritius, Kenya, Tanzania) range, advanced-only difficulty, and escape-risk warning. Used as the third source for cross-source averaging on tank size and temperature.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Originally assigned primary; demoted to additional because the LiveAquaria page identifies its 'Red Sea Moray Eel' as Gymnothorax javanicus rather than the target species G. griseus. Referenced here only to document the trade-name conflict in careNotes; husbandry numbers from this page (75 gal min tank, pH 8.1–8.2, SG 1.023–1.025, 74–84°F) were NOT used in the filed values because they describe a different species. The conflict itself is the most important fact captured from this source.
